title: GCD of an Array
description: You are given an array $$$a$$$ of length $$$n$$$. You are asked to process $$$q$$$ queries of the following format: given integers $$$i$$$ and $$$x$$$, multiply $$$a_i$$$ by $$$x$$$. After processing each query you need to output the greatest common divisor (GCD) of all elements of the array $$$a$$$. Since the answer can be too large, you are asked to output it modulo $$$10^9+7$$$.
input_spec: The first line contains two integers — $$$n$$$ and $$$q$$$ ($$$1 \le n, q \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$). The second line contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$) — the elements of the array $$$a$$$ before the changes. The next $$$q$$$ lines contain queries in the following format: each line contains two integers $$$i$$$ and $$$x$$$ ($$$1 \le i \le n$$$, $$$1 \le x \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$).
output_spec: Print $$$q$$$ lines: after processing each query output the GCD of all elements modulo $$$10^9+7$$$ on a separate line.

routes.name: Prime factorization with per-prime minimum tracking
routes.description: Decompose the GCD into independent per-prime problems: for each prime p, track the minimum exponent of p across all n elements, and maintain a running product as the answer.

observations.title: GCD decomposes into independent prime factors
observations.description: If each a_i has prime factorization a_i = p1^e1 * p2^e2 * ..., then gcd(a_1, ..., a_n) = product over all primes p of p^(min over all i of exponent of p in a_i). This means we can track each prime independently.

hints.nudge: What does the GCD look like when you write each number in terms of its prime factorization?
hints.question: If you know the exponent of prime p in every element a_i, what is the exponent of p in gcd(a_1, ..., a_n)?
hints.formal: gcd(a_1, ..., a_n) = product over primes p of p^(min_i(v_p(a_i))), where v_p(a_i) is the exponent of p in a_i. Each prime can be tracked independently.

observations.title: Multiplying only increases exponents, so the per-prime minimum can only increase
observations.description: Since each query multiplies a_i by x (never divides), the exponent of any prime p in any element can only grow or stay the same. Therefore the minimum exponent across all elements for prime p is monotonically non-decreasing over time. This means we never need to 'undo' a contribution to the answer — we only multiply new prime powers in.

hints.nudge: What direction can the GCD move when you only multiply elements?
hints.question: If v_p(a_i) can only increase over time, what can you say about how min_i(v_p(a_i)) changes?
hints.formal: Since v_p(a_i) is non-decreasing for every i and p, min_i(v_p(a_i)) is also non-decreasing. The answer (product of p^min) can only grow. Maintain a running answer and multiply in p each time the minimum exponent for p increases by 1.

observations.title: Track the minimum exponent efficiently with a multiset or sorted structure
observations.description: For each prime p, we need to quickly find the minimum exponent of p across all n elements. A multiset (or equivalent) keyed by exponent lets us update one element's exponent and read the new minimum in O(log n). But there is a subtlety: elements with exponent 0 for prime p need not be stored — the minimum is 0 (hence p does not contribute to GCD) as long as any element has exponent 0. The minimum is positive only once all n elements have a positive exponent.

hints.nudge: You need to quickly find the minimum exponent across n elements for a given prime, and update individual elements. What data structure supports this?
hints.question: If you store the exponents of prime p for all elements that have a positive exponent, when does the minimum become positive? How do you detect this transition?
hints.formal: For each prime p, maintain a multiset of the exponents v_p(a_i) for all i where v_p(a_i) > 0. The minimum exponent contributing to the GCD is: 0 if the multiset has fewer than n entries (some element has exponent 0), otherwise the smallest element in the multiset. When an update causes the multiset to reach size n, the new minimum is its smallest element.

observations.title: Use a sieve for fast factorization of values up to 2*10^5
observations.description: Both initial values and query multipliers are at most 2*10^5. Precompute the smallest prime factor (SPF) for every integer up to 2*10^5 using a sieve. Then any number can be factorized in O(log(value)) time by repeatedly dividing by its smallest prime factor.

hints.nudge: You need to factorize many numbers quickly. Can you precompute something to speed this up?
hints.question: If you know the smallest prime factor of every integer up to 2*10^5, how do you factorize a given number x?
hints.formal: Build an SPF sieve: for each i from 2 to max_val, if spf[i] is unset then spf[i] = i and for j = i*i, i*(i+1), ..., set spf[j] = i if unset. To factorize x: while x > 1, record p = spf[x], count how many times p divides x, then continue with x / p^count.

observations.title: Maintain the answer as a running product modulo 10^9+7
observations.description: Instead of recomputing the full GCD from scratch after each query, maintain a variable ans (initially 1) representing the current GCD modulo 10^9+7. Whenever the minimum exponent for some prime p increases from old_min to new_min, multiply ans by p^(new_min - old_min) mod 10^9+7. Since the minimum only increases, this always means multiplying in additional factors.

hints.nudge: Recomputing the GCD from all prime factors each query would be too slow. How can you update the answer incrementally?
hints.question: If the minimum exponent of prime p just increased by delta, how does the GCD change? How do you reflect this in a running modular product?
hints.formal: Keep ans = 1 (mod 10^9+7). After each update, for each prime p whose minimum exponent increased from old_min to new_min, set ans = ans * p^(new_min - old_min) mod 10^9+7. Since exponents only grow, old_min <= new_min always holds, so we only ever multiply (never divide), avoiding the need for modular inverse.

observations.title: Per-element prime maps avoid storing exponents for all (element, prime) pairs
observations.description: Naively storing exponents for every element-prime pair would be wasteful since most elements have few distinct prime factors. Instead, for each element i keep a map from prime to exponent (only primes that actually divide a_i). For each prime p keep a multiset of the nonzero exponents across elements. When processing query (i, x): factorize x, and for each prime factor p with additional exponent delta, update element i's map and the multiset for p.

hints.nudge: How many distinct prime factors can a number up to 2*10^5 have? Does this help with storage?
hints.question: If each number has at most about 6 distinct prime factors, what is the total work across all q queries for updating the per-element maps and per-prime multisets?
hints.formal: Each number up to 2*10^5 has at most 6 distinct prime factors (since 2*3*5*7*11*13*17 > 2*10^5). So each query touches O(log(max_val)) primes. Total operations across all queries: O((n + q) * log(max_val) * log(n)) where the log(n) comes from multiset operations. This comfortably fits in the 2.5 second time limit.
